channel.title: Expert Politics on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner
channel.description: Recent content in Expert Politics on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner

item.title: Chapter 13: Clairvoyants and Necromancers
item.description:
Chapter 13

Clairvoyants and Necromancers

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

Clairvoyants and Necromancers

Bob’s Journal entries, Sept 2000…

…The accepted “expert” today needs mainly to be a good cataloguer, and largely exists apart from “old fashioned” connoisseurship. If someone is able to collect into one catalogue, the entire body of an artist’s accepted work, the same diligent actuary is given authority to be an expert. The weakness inherent in this process is that the expert is not always a connoisseur or able to think outside the limited scope of standard literature on the artist. More important, he or she is unable to recognize un-catalogued new discoveries. Here one must be able to go beyond mere recognition and into the realm of synthesis.

item.title: Chapter 14: The Unknown Turner
item.description:
Chapter 14

The Unknown Turner

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

The Unknown Turner

Bob’s Journal Dec 2000…

Was a madman? John E. Anderson. Jr. had a thesis, several in fact. These were so far off the mark relative to traditional Turner scholarship that there could be no question. According to the way history has treated him, if not mad, he must have at least been a fool.

item.title: Chapter 15: Cool Britannia
item.description:
Chapter 15

Cool Britannia

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

Cool Britannia

A finely-finished topographical watercolour in Bob’s collection was framed and lettered by hand: “J.M.W. Turner R.A.” by Thos. Agnew and Son, years ago (see image No. 14). It has the best of provenances; it came from the estate of Turner’s friend, Rev. William Kingsley. Giving John Anderson’s theory about hidden signatures the benefit of the doubt, Bob searched long and hard for the mysterious cipher, and although his search was in vain he still refused to ignore the thirty-five years of dedicated effort Anderson applied to his study of JMW.

item.title: Chapter 16: Noblesse Oblige, or Not!
item.description:
Chapter 16

Noblesse Oblige, or Not!

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

Noblesse Oblige, or Not!

In young democratic , the work ethic was ablaze for . Things were evolving at a revolutionary pace, and it was not so much who you knew, but what you were able to accomplish by due diligence. People in were confident in their ability to succeed without the need to be connected or ennobled, and to progress at a pace commensurate with ability and effort. So entered the fray as an innocent. It is apparent from his actions that he mistakenly felt that after applying conscientious effort, he was able to blaze into the obdurate British art world and make his mark. Ah, but became a victim. As in Bob’s case, what doused his flame was the title-gazing inattention that often accompanies privilege? Fortunately for me, Bob was still smoldering.
